section.title: Future Glory
section.sectionRationale: Having declared believers to be heirs who suffer with Christ, Paul now lifts their eyes to the glory that awaits. Present sufferings are not worth comparing with the coming glory. All creation groans like a woman in labor, waiting for the revealing of the sons of God, and believers groan too, awaiting the redemption of their bodies. In their weakness the Spirit intercedes with groans too deep for words, and behind it all stands the assurance that God works all things together for good, in an unbreakable chain from His eternal foreknowledge to the final glory of His people.
section.thesis: The sufferings of this present time cannot be compared with the glory to be revealed; creation and believers groan in hope while the Spirit intercedes, and God works all things together for the good of those He foreknew, predestined, called, justified, and will surely glorify.
section.frameNote: Four movements: present sufferings weighed against future glory; the groaning of creation in hope; the groaning of believers and the interceding Spirit; and the golden chain by which God works all things for good, from foreknowledge to glory.

verses.text: I consider that our present sufferings are not comparable to the {{doxa|glory}} that will be revealed in us.

units.label: Creation groaning in hope
units.note: awaiting the revealing of the sons of God

verses.text: The creation waits in eager expectation for the revelation of the sons of God.
For the creation was subjected to futility, not by its own will, but because of the One who subjected it, in hope
that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay and brought into the glorious freedom of the children of God.
We know that the whole creation has been {{stenazo|groaning}} together in the pains of childbirth until the present time.

units.label: Believers groaning and the interceding Spirit
units.note: the firstfruits of the Spirit, hope, and help in weakness

verses.text: Not only that, but we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for our adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies.
For in this {{elpis|hope}} we were saved; but hope that is seen is no hope at all. Who hopes for what he can already see?
But if we hope for what we do not yet see, we wait for it patiently.
In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness. For we do not know how we ought to pray, but the Spirit Himself intercedes for us with groans too deep for words.
And He who searches our hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to the will of God.

units.label: The golden chain, from foreknowledge to glory
units.note: God works all things together for good

verses.text: And we know that God works all things together for the good of those who love Him, who are called according to His purpose.
For those God {{proginosko|foreknew}}, He also {{proorizo|predestined}} to be conformed to the image of His Son, so that He would be the firstborn among many brothers.
And those He predestined, He also called; those He called, He also justified; those He justified, He also glorified.

doxa.gloss: Glory, radiance, weight of splendor. It is the shining honor and likeness of God Himself, into which His children will be brought. Paul weighs all present suffering against this coming glory and finds no comparison; the glory to be revealed in us so far outweighs our afflictions that they seem light and momentary beside it.

stenazo.gloss: To groan, to sigh under a burden. Paul hears three groans in this passage: the groaning of creation in the pains of childbirth, the inward groaning of believers who await the redemption of their bodies, and the wordless groans of the Spirit interceding within them. The groan is not despair but the ache of hope, longing for the birth of the new world.

elpis.gloss: Hope, confident expectation of a certain future good. Biblical hope is not wishful thinking but sure assurance grounded in God's promise. In this hope believers were saved, and because it reaches to what is not yet seen, it is held with patient endurance while creation and the children of God await their freedom.

proginosko.gloss: To know beforehand. In Scripture God's knowing is often more than foresight of facts; it is His setting of covenant love upon persons, as when He says He knew Israel of all the families of the earth. Those God foreknew He marked out as His own before time, the first link in the unbreakable chain that ends in glory.

proorizo.gloss: To mark out beforehand, to predestine, to appoint in advance to a certain end. God predestined those He foreknew to be conformed to the image of His Son. The word secures that salvation begins in God's eternal purpose, not in human decision, and moves surely toward its appointed goal, the likeness of Christ and the sharing of His glory.

basic.context: Paul has just called believers heirs who suffer with Christ before being glorified with Him. Now he opens up that glory. The whole world is broken and groaning, and Christians groan too, but not without hope. The Spirit prays within us when we do not know how, and God is working every single thing toward the good of His people and their final glory.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as comfort for sufferers. Paul does not deny the pain; he weighs it against the coming glory and finds it light by comparison. The three groans, creation, believers, and the Spirit, hold together honesty about suffering and confidence in hope. The chain of verses 29 and 30 is meant to steady trembling hearts: what God begins in eternity He will surely finish in glory.
basic.originalLanguages: The coming splendor is doxa, glory, the weight of God's own radiance. The groaning is stenazo, the ache of creation, of believers, and of the Spirit. Believers were saved in elpis, sure hope for what is not yet seen. And the chain begins with those God did proginosko, foreknew, and proorizo, predestined, to be conformed to His Son.
basic.typology: The bondage of creation to decay recalls the ground cursed after the fall, and its coming freedom answers that curse. The firstfruits of the Spirit recalls the firstfruits offered to God as a pledge of the full harvest. The redemption of the body is the true and final exodus, the setting free of the whole person and the whole world from bondage.
basic.christ: The goal of predestination is to be conformed to the image of God's Son, that He might be the firstborn among many brothers. Christ is the pattern of the glory to come and the elder brother of the redeemed family. The Spirit who intercedes is His Spirit, and the good toward which all things work is our being made like Him and brought to share His glory.
basic.god: God is sovereign over suffering and history: He subjected creation in hope, He works all things together for good, and He carries His people from foreknowledge to glory without losing one. He is the God who searches hearts, hears the Spirit's intercession, and purposes the good of those who love Him, a good defined as likeness to His Son.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Corinthians 4 weighs light momentary affliction against an eternal weight of glory. Genesis 3 tells of the ground cursed, which creation's groaning recalls. Ephesians 1 speaks of being predestined in love before the foundation of the world. Philippians 1 promises that God will complete the work He began.
basic.oneStory: The whole creation has been groaning since the fall, longing to be set free. Paul tells the story's end: God is bringing many sons to glory, conformed to the image of His Son, and the redeemed creation itself will share their freedom. From God's eternal purpose to the final glory, the story cannot fail, for God Himself carries it through.

context.literary: This section flows directly out of verse 17, where suffering and glory were joined. Paul develops the glory side at length, structuring the passage around three groans and ending with the chain of five verbs, foreknew, predestined, called, justified, glorified, that carries the reader from eternity past to eternity future. The tone rises from present ache to unshakable assurance.
context.historical: Paul writes to believers who faced real hardship in Rome and would soon face far worse under Nero. The teaching that present sufferings are not worth comparing with future glory, and that God works all things for good, was not abstract theory but strength for a persecuted church learning to wait in hope.
context.canonical: This passage joins the Bible's story of a cursed creation awaiting renewal, from Genesis 3 to Revelation 21. Its language of predestination stands alongside Ephesians 1 and John 6, and its assurance that God completes what He begins echoes across the New Testament. Romans 8:28 to 30 has anchored the doctrine of God's sovereign, saving purpose.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort suffering believers by setting their present afflictions against the incomparable glory to come, to teach them that creation and they themselves groan in hope while the Spirit helps them pray, and to ground their assurance in God's unbreakable purpose that carries His people from eternal foreknowledge to final glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the state of creation, the groaning of believers, the ministry of the Spirit, and the saving purpose of God. Its abiding call is prescriptive: to wait patiently in hope, to trust that God works all things for good, and to rest in the certainty of His purpose rather than in circumstances.

debates.question: What does it mean that God foreknew and predestined those who are His?

views.label: Foreknowledge as God's electing love
views.case: The Reformed reading, to be foregrounded, holds that God's foreknowing is His covenantal setting of love upon persons, not a bare foresight of facts; as He says He knew Israel of all the families of the earth, so He foreknew His people, and this electing love grounds an unconditional predestination that begins the golden chain and cannot fail.

views.label: Foreknowledge as foreseen faith
views.case: Others hold that God foreknew who would believe and on that basis predestined them, so that His choice takes account of the faith He foresees in them.

debates.bearing: Both agree that the chain from foreknowledge to glory is unbroken and that the believer's final glory is utterly secure in God; the Reformed reading, here foregrounded, roots that security wholly in God's prior electing love rather than in anything He foresees in us, giving the deepest assurance.

debates.question: Does all things work together for good mean believers will be spared hardship?

views.label: All things serve the good of conformity to Christ
views.case: The historic reading takes the good as being conformed to the image of God's Son, verse 29, so that even suffering and loss are woven by God into that saving purpose; it is not a promise of ease but of a certain, Christ-shaped good.

views.label: A promise of favorable outcomes
views.case: Some hear it as a pledge that circumstances will turn out pleasantly, which the surrounding talk of suffering and groaning does not support.

debates.bearing: The context defines the good as likeness to Christ and final glory, so the verse comforts sufferers not by removing hardship but by assuring them that God bends everything, even pain, toward their ultimate good in His Son.

study.originalLanguages: The horizon of the passage is doxa, glory, the weight of God's own radiance into which His children will be brought, so vast that present suffering cannot be weighed against it. Running through it is stenazo, groaning: creation groans in the pains of childbirth, believers groan inwardly for the redemption of their bodies, and the Spirit groans in intercession too deep for words. Believers live by elpis, confident hope for what is not yet seen, held with patient endurance. And the ground of it all is that those God did proginosko, foreknew, He also did proorizo, predestined, to be conformed to the image of His Son, so that the whole chain of salvation rests on God's eternal purpose.

typology.fulfillment: Recalls the ground cursed after the fall in Genesis 3 and finds its answer in the coming freedom of the whole creation, renewed with the children of God.
Like the firstfruits offered to God as a pledge of the full harvest, the Spirit given now is the guarantee of the full redemption of the body.
The true and final exodus, the setting free of the whole person from bondage to decay, fulfilling every deliverance God has ever worked for His people.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center of the future glory. God's saving purpose is to conform His people to the image of His Son, so that Christ might be the firstborn among many brothers, the pattern and head of a great redeemed family. The glory that will be revealed is the glory believers share with Him. The Spirit who intercedes is the Spirit of Christ, and the good toward which God works all things is nothing less than being made like Christ and glorified with Him. He is the goal of predestination and the elder brother of the saved.
study.god: This passage displays the sovereignty and faithfulness of God. He subjected creation to futility yet in hope, He works all things together for the good of those who love Him, and He carries His people through an unbroken chain, foreknew, predestined, called, justified, glorified, without losing a single one. He searches hearts and hears the wordless intercession of His Spirit. Behind all the groaning of a broken world stands a God who reigns over it and is bringing His children surely to glory, so that their hope can never be put to shame.

crossReferences.note: Light and momentary affliction is producing an eternal weight of glory, closely echoing the weighing of suffering against glory here.
The ground is cursed because of sin, the bondage to decay that creation's groaning recalls and that its coming freedom answers.
God chose and predestined His people in love before the foundation of the world, parallel to foreknew and predestined here.
He who began a good work will complete it, matching the certainty of the chain from foreknowledge to glory.

study.oneStory: Since the fall the whole creation has groaned under the curse, longing to be set free. Paul unveils the end of the story: God is bringing many sons to glory, conformed to the image of His Son, and the creation itself will share the freedom of the children of God. From God's eternal foreknowledge, through calling and justification, to final glory, the saving purpose runs unbroken, so that the ache of the present is swallowed up in a hope that cannot fail.

quiz.q: How does Paul weigh present sufferings against future glory?

options.text: Present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us.
options.note: Verse 18 declares the coming glory so great that suffering cannot be weighed against it.

options.text: Present sufferings and future glory are roughly equal.
options.note: Closer: they can be set side by side, but Paul says they are not comparable, the glory far outweighing the suffering.

options.text: Present sufferings prove that God has abandoned His people.
options.note: Paul treats suffering as the path to glory, not a sign of abandonment.

options.text: Future glory is uncertain and may never come.
options.note: The glory is certain; the whole passage rests on its sure hope.

quiz.q: Why does creation groan, according to Paul?

options.text: It was subjected to futility in hope, and groans in the pains of childbirth awaiting its freedom.
options.note: Verses 20 to 22 describe creation groaning in hope for its coming liberation.

options.text: It groans in final despair, with no hope of renewal.
options.note: Closer: the groaning is real, but it is in hope; creation will be set free from bondage to decay.

options.text: It groans because matter is inherently evil.
options.note: Creation is not evil but subjected to futility, and it will be renewed, not destroyed.

options.text: It does not really groan; this is only a figure with no meaning.
options.note: Paul teaches a real longing of creation for the revealing of the sons of God.

quiz.q: How does the Spirit help believers in their weakness?

options.text: He intercedes for them with groans too deep for words, according to the will of God.
options.note: Verses 26 and 27 describe the Spirit's intercession when we do not know how to pray.

options.text: He removes all weakness so that prayer becomes easy.
options.note: The Spirit helps us in our weakness rather than abolishing it.

options.text: He prays instead of us so that we need not pray at all.
options.note: Closer: the Spirit truly intercedes, but He helps alongside our praying, not in place of it.

options.text: He leaves believers to manage their prayers on their own.
options.note: The whole point is that the Spirit Himself intercedes for us.

quiz.q: What is the good that God works all things together for?

options.text: The good of being conformed to the image of His Son and finally glorified.
options.note: Verse 29 defines the good as conformity to Christ, the goal of God's purpose.

options.text: A comfortable life free from all hardship.
options.note: Closer: God does work for our good, but the good is Christlikeness and glory, not ease, since the context is suffering.

options.text: Immediate success in every earthly endeavor.
options.note: The good is defined by the passage as likeness to Christ, not worldly success.

options.text: Nothing in particular; the phrase is only a comforting slogan.
options.note: Paul gives it precise content: conformity to the Son and final glory.

quiz.q: What is the golden chain of verses 29 and 30?

options.text: Those God foreknew He predestined, called, justified, and glorified, an unbroken saving purpose.
options.note: The five verbs carry God's people from eternity to glory without loss.

options.text: A series of steps believers must climb by their own effort.
options.note: God is the actor in every link; it is His work, not human striving.

options.text: A chain that can be broken at any point by failure.
options.note: Closer: the chain is real, but it is unbroken; all whom God foreknew He also glorified.

options.text: A promise that applies only to the apostles.
options.note: It applies to all who love God and are called according to His purpose.

questions: Paul says present sufferings are not worth comparing with the coming glory. How would keeping that comparison before you change the way you carry a present hardship?
All creation groans in hope for renewal. How does it enlarge your hope to know that God will not scrap the world but set it free from bondage to decay?
The Spirit intercedes for you with groans too deep for words. What comfort is there in knowing that even when you cannot pray, the Spirit is praying within you according to God's will?
God works all things together for the good of conforming you to Christ. Where do you need to trust that even painful things are being woven toward that good?
From foreknowledge to glory, God loses none of His own. How does the certainty of that chain steady you when your assurance feels shaky?

translationNotes.note: Futility renders a word meaning emptiness or frustration, the inability of creation to reach its intended purpose under the curse. Because of the One who subjected it points to God, who subjected creation not in anger only but in hope of its coming renewal.

God works all things together for good can also be read all things work together for good; the better attested reading makes God the active worker. The good is not ease but the conformity to Christ named in verse 29.

Foreknew renders proginosko; in biblical usage God's knowing often means His covenantal setting of love upon persons, not merely foresight of facts. Predestined renders proorizo, to mark out beforehand for an appointed end, here conformity to the image of His Son.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 8:18-30. Footnote markers were omitted and line breaks treated as spaces per the source structure. No em dashes required substitution in this passage. Foreknowledge and predestination are treated with the Reformed electing-love reading foregrounded and the foreseen-faith view represented fairly, both grounding the security of the believer. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
